He’s a visionary in tech but also swings markets on a whim. I’d give him low-to-moderate trust on Bitcoin specifically—he may drive short-term moves but I doubt he’s making a sober, data-driven forecast for price in 5–10 years.  

2. Nouriel Roubini  
  1. Do I know who he is?  
     – Vaguely.  
  2. Would I look him up? If yes, what would I find and how would that change my view?  
     – Yes, I’d Google him. I’d learn he’s an NYU economics professor nicknamed “Dr. Doom” for forecasting the 2008 crisis and that he’s been extremely bearish on crypto, calling it a bubble. That background would give him some credibility on downside risk—he’s an academic economist, not a self-interested crypto promoter—but he tends to be very pessimistic. I’d take his $2,000 forecast as a red flag (“maybe he’s too negative”), though it reminds me there’s real downside if adoption stalls.  

3. Warren Buffett  
  1. Do I know who he is?  
     – Yes.  
  2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment?  
     – He’s the longtime value-investing legend at Berkshire Hathaway. He famously refuses to touch crypto, calling it speculative and non-productive. I respect his track record on companies and fundamentals, so I’d give him moderate-to-high trust on market valuation—but I also know he doesn’t like new asset classes outside his circle of competence. His $15,000 forecast reflects that deep-value lens; it feels plausible if Bitcoin never becomes “productive,” but I’d weigh it alongside other views on mainstream adoption.  

4. Michael Saylor  
  1. Do I know who he is?  
     – Vaguely.  
  2. Would I look him up? If yes, what would I find and how would that change my view?  
     – Yes, I’d check him out. I’d discover he’s the executive chairman of MicroStrategy, has put billions of corporate cash into Bitcoin, and is one of its most vocal evangelists. I’d view his $1 million prediction with skepticism, discount it heavily, and treat it more as marketing than an objective estimate.

I respect his engineering background and vision for innovation, but when it comes to finance or Bitcoin price forecasting, I take his numbers with a big grain of salt. I’d say I trust him “somewhat” to identify emerging trends, but his specific price target feels more like a promotional anchor than a rigorous forecast.

Nouriel Roubini  
1. Do I know who this is? Vaguely.  
2. If yes—what do I know and trust?  
   – I know him as the “Dr. Doom” economist who warned about the 2008 financial crisis and is famously bearish on crypto, calling it a bubble. I don’t follow him closely, but I’ve seen headlines quoting him saying Bitcoin has no intrinsic value. I respect that he’s a bona fide academic and policymaker, but his blanket skepticism sometimes comes off as ideological. I’d give him moderate credibility on systemic risk and bubbles, but I wouldn’t assume his $2,000 price call is a precise estimate—more that he’s signaling “this is overhyped.”

Warren Buffett  
1. Do I know who this is? Yes.  
2. What do I know and how much do I trust him?  
   – I know him as the legendary value investor—CEO of Berkshire Hathaway, long‐term track record of picking productive businesses, and famously dismissive of crypto (“it produces nothing”). I trust his judgment on fundamental value and long-run returns better than almost anyone. At the same time, I also recognize he’s very old-school—he only invests in cash-flowing assets and may be slow to adapt to genuinely new asset classes. His $15,000 prediction, to me, says “I think Bitcoin will fade into insignificance,” and that carries weight, but I’d consider it a floor value from a value-investor lens rather than a crystal-ball forecast.

Michael Saylor  
1. Do I know who this is? No.  
2. Would I look him up? Yes. If I did, I’d find that he’s the CEO or executive chairman of MicroStrategy, a business‐intelligence software company that’s converted large chunks of its treasury into Bitcoin. He’s arguably the most vocal institutional bull, framing Bitcoin as “digital energy” and urging other companies to follow suit. Knowing that, I’d see he has massive skin in the game—and probably a strong incentive to push prices higher. That doesn’t automatically invalidate his view, but I’d mentally discount it as highly biased, promotional, and probably less about independent price discovery than about shoring up confidence among shareholders who ride or die with MicroStrategy’s balance sheet.

Here’s how I’d reflect on each of these voices before sketching out my own view. I’ve tried to be honest about what I know off the top of my head, how much I’d trust each, and whether I’d dig in for more background.

1. Cathie Wood ($500K by end-2025)  
   1. Do I know who she is?  
      – Vaguely. I’ve seen her name in financial news and follow ARK Invest’s newsletters sometimes.  
   2. If yes – what do I know and how much do I trust her on Bitcoin/markets?  
      – I know she runs ARK Invest, is known for very bullish calls on disruptive tech (Tesla, crypto, genomics) and for publishing transparent “model” projections. Her funds can be wildly volatile but often capture big up-swings.  
      – Trust level: moderate (around 3/5). I respect that she builds quantitative models, but I also know her targets can be super aggressive and don’t always pan out on schedule.  
   3. If no/vaguely – would I look her up?  
      – I already have a rough sense. I wouldn’t dig deeper right away unless I was putting real money in—or unless her forecast wandered even higher.

2. Michael Saylor ($1,000K)  
   1. Do I know who he is?  
      – Yes. He’s the CEO of MicroStrategy who’s famously bought billions of dollars’ worth of Bitcoin as a corporate treasury asset.  
   2. What do I know and how much do I trust him?  
      – I know he worships Bitcoin almost in religious terms (“digital energy,” “apex property of the human race”). He’s extremely bullish, almost too certain. He frames halvings as rocket fuel and cites network effects.  
      – Trust level: low-to-moderate (about 2.5/5). I respect his conviction but worry he’s overleveraged in his own company and prone to hype. I wouldn’t take his seven-figure target at face value without seeing a broader range of views.  
   3. (not applicable – I already know him)

3. Jamie Dimon ($5K)  
   1. Do I know who he is?  
      – Yes. He’s the long-time CEO of JPMorgan Chase. A household name in banking.  
   2. What do I know and how much do I trust his judgment on Bitcoin?  
      – I know he’s publicly called Bitcoin a “fraud” or “pet rock,” and that big banks often see crypto as a threat to their fees. He’s very influential but clearly biased against anything that undermines traditional finance.  
      – Trust level: moderate (3/5), but with a big pinch of salt. He knows markets cold, but he has a vested interest in downplaying crypto’s legitimacy.  
   3. (not applicable)

4. Peter Schiff ($3K)  
   1. Do I know who he is?  
      – Vaguely. I’ve heard his name on YouTube and seen him debate crypto advocates on podcasts.  
   2. If yes – what do I know and how much do I trust him?  
      – I know he’s a gold-bug, runs Euro Pacific Capital, and is almost aggressively bearish on Bitcoin, forecasting crashes whenever there’s any downturn. He values gold’s centuries-long history versus crypto’s youth.  
      – Trust level: fairly low (around 1.5/5). He’s consistent but often seems dogmatic—his anti-crypto stance doesn’t leave much room for nuance.  
   3. If no – would I look him up before weighing his call?  
      – Yes, I’d probably Google “Peter Schiff background” to remind myself of his track record and any major hits or misses. I’d find that he called a 2020–21 crash that never came, that he correctly warned about the 2008 housing bubble but then has been hyper-bearish since 2010. That history would reinforce my skepticism of a $3K “crash” call timed to 2025.

Bottom line:  
– Cathie Wood: I give her props for a transparent, model-based approach but temper expectations.  
– Michael Saylor: Too much zeal, too one-sided—interesting perspective but not my anchor.  
– Jamie Dimon: Influential voice, but a clear conflict with crypto; he’s not neutral.  
– Peter Schiff: Distrustful of his one-track gold bias; I’d double-check his specific projections and miss-rate.
